data.title: Standard Lithium: Positioned At The Crossroads Of Geology, Tech, And U.S. Strategy
data.description:
A strategic bet beyond traditional mining

Standard Lithium Ltd. (NYSE:SLI) represents a strategic bet within the North American energy transition ecosystem. Rather than repeating the traditional lithium mining model, the company has designed a proposal that combines technological innovation, institutional support, and world-class geological resources.

With projects located in the industrial heart of the U.S., partnerships with giants like Equinor (EQNR) and Koch Industries, and direct federal subsidies, the company is building a platform to produce lithium at low cost, low environmental impact, and high added value.

Over the last few quarters, SLI has validated its direct extraction technology (DLE), closed key deals and consolidated its capitalization without the need for debt or massive dilution. Although it does not yet generate operating revenue, its profile resembles that of an industrial startup with leadership ambitions, rather than that of a traditional mining company in the exploratory phase.

In this context, I believe that for risk-tolerant and long-term investors, SLI offers a rare combination of structural upside and strategic alignment with U.S. industrial priorities.

World-class resources in the industrial heart of the U.S.

From a technical point of view, the heart of the project is located in the Smackover geological formation, a brine belt running through Arkansas and Texas.

In this region, the company has reported the highest lithium brine concentrations in North America, reaching a record 806 mg/L in Texas and 616 mg/L at the recently completed Lester well of the SWA Project.

This difference is critical, as brine quality directly impacts efficiency, CAPEX and positioning in the overall cost curve.

Technical validation that exceeds global standards

Technology validation is no less important. In March 2025, Standard Lithium successfully completed the field-test of its DLE process using Li-Pro technology developed by Koch Technology Solutions.

The result was 95.4% recovery efficiency, over 99% impurity rejection and more than 11,000 accumulated operating cycles.

This experience is leveraged in the demonstration plant that has been operating since 2020, through which more than 31MM gallons of brine have passed.

A project supported by grants and institutional partnerships

In terms of planning, the South West Arkansas (SWA) project aims to produce 45,000 tons/year of lithium carbonate in two phases. The final design is being refined through FEED and DFS studies, with a final investment decision (FID) scheduled for before the end of the year.

The project was already included in the Federal Permitting Dashboard and received a $225MM grant from the U.S. Department of Energy, one of the largest grants to a lithium company in the country.

Healthy capitalization and undiluted funding

At a strategic level, the company has partnered with Equinor (EQNR), which provides financing, underground know-how and onshore project execution.

This company was structured without issuing new shares of the parent, which reinforces the thesis that SLI has prioritized non-dilutive forms of financing, such as public subsidies, future offtakes, and project-level debt.

All this with an extremely healthy capital structure: zero debt, $31.6MM in cash, and a market capitalization of $507MM at the end of March 2025.

Valuation and multiples: an early reading of the potential

From the valuation point of view, traditional multiples are not yet representative. The company has no operating income or EBITDA, so most financial ratios appear as "NM." However, some indicators provide clear benchmarks.

From a profitability perspective, the operating flow remains negative (-$24.9 MM), which is consistent with its intensive investment phase. The ROE appears artificially high due to accounting effects, but does not reflect a real return on capital.

In contrast, the growth of working capital (+39.9%) and the low monthly burn-rate reinforce the thesis of financial sustainability in the short term.

Momentum signals outperformance across all time frames

SLI’s recent performance has been outstanding and supports its Momentum Grade A rating within the materials sector.

In the last three months, the stock has advanced 76.8%, while in the last year it accumulated a rise of 128.2%, exceeding by a large margin both the sector average and its own historical performance.

This trend is also reflected when compared to the S&P 500 (SP500): in the last twelve months, SLI gained 143.6% against 21.0% of the index, with a positive difference of more than 120 pp.

The momentum has been especially intensified in H1 2025, driven by key news such as the agreement with EQNR, the award of a federal DOE grant and the validation of its DLE technology, factors that have raised market expectations about its strategic role in the lithium supply chain in the U.S.

The market pushes, geography accompanies

The market opportunity is equally relevant. The projected structural lithium deficit in the U.S. by 2030, combined with the nearshoring of the battery value chain, makes SLI a well-positioned player to capitalize on industrial relocation.

Its proximity to manufacturing centers such as Texas, Tennessee, and Michigan substantially reduces logistics times and the carbon footprint of your final product.

Conclusion: a strategic position with pending execution

SLI is not a company for impatient investors. But for those who understand that large returns are born in the pre-production stages, and that the real risk lies in the technological and commercial execution, the company offers one of the best risk-return asymmetries in the sector.

With validated technology, grants awarded, world-class institutional partners, and a top-tier resource in the U.S., I have no doubt that the company is positioned to scale quickly once an investment decision has been made. Until then, keeping an eye on the next milestones will be key.

Given the company’s exceptional momentum, strategic positioning, and upcoming catalysts, I consider SLI a speculative Buy option for investors with high risk tolerance, with potential for additional revaluation as execution progresses.

data.title: Precipio: Cash Flow Positivity May Remove Going Concern Language
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Deep Sail Capital Partner Q2 2025 Investor Letter.

Precipio (NASDAQ:PRPO) is a microcap specialty cancer diagnostics company offering both molecular assay kits and pathology services. Its core product lines include HemeScreen molecular assays, and IV-Cell Culture media, as well as operating its own clinical laboratory. HemeScreen is a set of proprietary molecular tests for hematologic malignancies (i.e., blood cancers). IV-Cell culture media is a universal cytogenetic culture media used for testing of hematologic cancers (i.e., Leukemia). Both products utilize proprietary chemical assays that have been developed over the years and are kept as trade secrets by the company, providing them protection from anyone trying to copy their products. Precipio’s estimated market share remains under 1% of its total addressable market in their current products. Both product end markets are growing within the broader $115B oncology diagnostics market. With the help of their key product distributors, including Thermo Fisher, McKesson (MCK), Medline, and Cardinal Health (CAH), the company has an excellent path to growth within their current product portfolio and the ability to launch similar new cancer diagnostic products into adjacent markets.

The company has been on a multi-year path to get to cashflow and EBITDA breakeven. Through a combination of cost-cutting efforts and scaling efficiencies, they have been able to improve their pathology services gross margins from the mid-20s to the mid-40s over the last 3 years. CEO Ilan Danieli noted in their Q4 2024 results that they are past the point of needing outside capital, saying, “Managing a company that no longer relies on outside capital infusions for its survival enables us to make decisions based on growth and value creation without the constraints imposed by cash conservation concerns.” The last three quarters further underscore their progress toward a positive cash flow, in which in each quarter they generated near zero EBITDA.

In the last 2 years there have been two industry-related issues that have negatively impacted their business, which seem to be behind the company. Firstly, there was a hack at Change Healthcare, the country’s largest clearinghouse which facilitates the majority of insurance billing nationwide. In February of 2024 that hack caused significant billing and payment delays into late 2024 for most healthcare providers in the country. The company relied on Change Healthcare’s temporary funding assistance program in 2024 to help stabilize the company’s balance sheet. This hack was resolved in late 2024, and the cash flow from payors that Precipio relies on has been fully restored. Secondly, in late 2024, the FDA provided a final rule regulating laboratory development tests (LDTs) as medical devices, which could have negatively impacted the entire diagnostic field’s regulatory requirements by imposing extremely stringent, complex and costly regulations on laboratories in the US. This caused significant uncertainty among laboratories as to their ability to purchase assays such as HemeScreen, and as a result, many laboratories placed on hold all efforts to explore new products, impacting Precipio’s Products Division growth. The rule was struck down in Q1 by a federal court in Texas, which vacated the rule, making it no longer legally binding. This uncertainty in the industry has been lifted from the company, and shortly after, Precipio saw numerous laboratories resume their interest in the company’s products.

Precipio currently has "going concern" language in their financial audit due to recurring operating losses and concerns about its ability to meet financial obligations without raising additional capital. This language reflected historical cash burn and uncertainty exacerbated by external disruptions such as the Change Healthcare cyberattack and the FDA ruling. However, in its most recent quarters, the company has demonstrated a meaningful shift toward financial stability, reporting positive operating cash flow and significantly improved revenue performance. On a recent call I had with the company’s CEO, Ilan Danieli, he expressed a belief that continued cash flow positivity, combined with cost controls and increased customer demand, strengthens Precipio’s financial position and will likely lead to the removal of the going concern language in upcoming filings.

Product Overview

Precipio delivers a dual offering of molecular assay kits and pathology services through its CLIA certified laboratory in New Haven, Connecticut. Its core product lines include HemeScreen and Bloodhound—proprietary molecular panels designed for the rapid and accurate detection of hematologic malignancies such as CML, MPN, and AML. It has also developed IV-Cell, a universal cytogenetics culture media which helps laboratories diagnosing blood cancers achieve higher accuracy and efficiency in their cytogenetics lab. These products are sold primarily as lab developed test (LDT) kits to physician offices and reference labs in the heme space. In parallel, Precipio’s in-house CAP/CLIA-certified lab ("Pathology Services") conducts clinical testing, driving recurring revenue and supporting ongoing R&D. Between the two products, the main focus of the business is overwhelmingly HemeScreen and has been the focus of the sales organization due to the higher margins (~70% vs 40%) and more impactful revenue per customer of that product.

In early 2025, Precipio received MolDx (Medicare) approval for its NGS cancer panel, a major milestone that enables direct Medicare billing in certain states. This change is expected to generate approximately $250,000 in 100% margin (due to the fact the tests were already being run previous to this approval) revenue per quarter, or $1 million annually, as it monetizes tests the company was already performing, previously without getting paid.

From a competitive standpoint, Precipio's offerings stand out for their speed, simplicity, and affordability. While narrower in scope than some broader NGS panels, HemeScreen provides clinically actionable results faster than traditional NGS and is easier to run than manual PCR— making it ideal for clinical labs that are focused on providing quick and accurate results to patients, vs research institutions.

Similarly, IV-Cell occupies a specialized but valuable niche within cytogenetics, offering higher success rates and better efficiency at a competitive price point compared to other culture media solutions.

Both product lines have demonstrated strong product-market fit, delivering clear value to their target customers. Precipio's current market share remains small relative to the broader cancer diagnostics landscape, but recent distributor partnerships position the company to expand meaningfully. The global oncology diagnostics market was valued at approximately $115 billion in 2025 and is growing at a 6% CAGR. Within this space, Precipio targets a focused subsegment—hematologic cancer diagnostics in physician-office and reference laboratories that remains underserved by major players. While competition includes national labs like LabCorp and Quest (DGX), as well as specialized kit makers like Thermo Fisher, Illumina, and Qiagen, Precipio's differentiated assays and strong provider relationships offer a defensible niche.

Importantly, the FDA’s proposed 2024 crackdown on LDTs, which could have disrupted Precipio’s model, was reversed in 2025, removing a key regulatory overhang. In summary, while large IVD players compete in broad markets, Precipio is positioned to grow within a distinct and expanding niche—offering a compelling opportunity in leukemia and hematologic cancer diagnostics.

High-Quality Business Model

  • Recurring, high-margin revenues: The pathology services division supplies specialized cancer tests reimbursed by insurers, providing stable recurring revenues. In Q1 2025, pathology volumes grew 46% YoY, driving service revenue to increase by 53%. Product (kit) sales are also growing and yield higher margins. Precipio’s overall gross margin has expanded significantly (≈27% in 2024 to ~43% in Q1 2025) due to higher volumes and efficiency. Notably, pathology gross margins jumped from 24% to 42% YoY and product margins from 37% to 51%, reflecting strong leverage driven by both their cost-cutting efforts and the scalability of their laboratory. The company has expressed that their laboratory has significant additional capacity to support ongoing growth; and that Product margins too have significant room to growth due to production scale economies.
  • Differentiated expertise and R&D model: Precipio leverages its case volume to selffund innovation. In 2024 the lab processed over 12,000 specimens, effectively creating a “self-financed” R&D pipeline for new assays. The company’s close integration with academic pathologists and focus on hard-to-diagnose cancers provides a moat: few competitors offer the same combined molecular/histology expertise. Proprietary IP (HemeScreen trade secrets, unique panel designs), hard-to-replicate culture media, and distribution partnerships further extend reach.
  • Cost discipline: Management emphasizes tight cost control. Q1 adjusted EBITDA loss shrank 92% YoY to only $108K, and cash burn fell 93%. The firm has not raised equity in 2024 and is focused on returning to positive cash flow by mid-2025, which we expect to occur in Q3 post the MolDx approval. This disciplined approach has allowed accelerating top-line growth (~53% YoY in pathology revenues) without a proportional increase in SG&A (sales staff headcount was flat despite ~50% revenue growth). Management has said that by leveraging outside distributors and the existing sales team, they expect to continue to grow revenues at a high rate without the need to materially expand their sales team.

Management Team Review

Founder/CEO Ilan Danieli has led Precipio since its 2011 inception. Under his leadership, the company has steadily scaled their revenues. The company went public in 2018 on a very small IPO, raising only $6 million. Since then, the company has raised money via equity issuance only two times, once via direct offering in 2023 for $2m and once via an ATM in 2024 for $0.1m. The company has a current ATM in place for $6m, but they have only exercised $0.1m. Ilan has only raised money when he has absolutely had to keep the business moving forward. In recent quarters the management team has delivered robust pathology growth (+53% YoY) while dramatically improving their cash burn through a combination of cost controls and sales improvements. Management has prioritized capital efficiency, including declining to do a dilutive equity raise in 2024 after the Change Healthcare cyberattack when they were in need of cash, relying instead on improved operations and one-time funding (e.g., CARES Act credits and Change Healthcare temporary funding assistance). Importantly, management ties compensation to performance—options issued in 2025 vest based on share price milestones as opposed to commonly issued time-based vesting options —which aligns management interests with shareholders. Overall, the team’s track record of hitting operational targets and their commitment to avoid shareholder dilution give confidence in execution.

Long-Term Growth Prospects

Precipio has multiple growth catalysts, both short-term and long-term, that will drive the business into a profitable growth phase over the next 3 to 5 years.

Medicare reimbursement (MolDx): In Q1 2025 the company secured MolDx approval for its NGS panel, unlocking ~$250K per quarter in incremental revenue on existing test volume. This change will begin to show up in Q2 revenue and net income. Due to the fact this is additional revenue from existing test volumes that were already being run, the revenue will be 100% GM and fall directly to the bottom line. This alone will take the company from roughly cash flow neutral to generating $1M/year in free cash flow.

Pathology volume growth: Physician uptake is strong (11 new physicians in Q1 of 2025). Volumes jumped 46% YoY and revenues were up 53% YoY, and management expects a continued rebound in H2 after seasonal headwinds that occur in Q1 & Q2 in their business. Once physicians adopt the tests, they have a low churn rate and tend to continue to use the tests on a go-forward basis, so the expectation is this growth in pathology is not a one-off and is sustainable into the future.

Product pipeline: A large oncology lab has committed to 5 of Precipio’s 6 panels, potentially adding low seven-figure annual sales once fully live. Several distributors in the US have introduced customers that are in early validation stages for upcoming panels, implying further rollouts. By owning and operating their own laboratory, Precipio is able to better control R&D spending and test new reagents and panels in a cost-effective way. As the company has now achieved its short-term goal of becoming self-funded, they will shift their focus to expanding their product panel offering in the coming years.

Combining these growth drivers, I believe that it is fair to model healthy multi-year growth for Precipio. Using the Q1 2025 run rate (~$20M annualized) as a base, a mid-20% annual CAGR (which is their average revenue growth over the LTM) over three years would take revenue to ~$35M by FY2028. But if you add revenue for the $1m from the MolDx approval and adjust for Q1 seasonality, a more realistic revenue forecast for 2025 is $26-28m.

For context, larger peers are seeing growth rates in the high teens to mid-30s, including Guardant (GH), which targets ~18% revenue growth in 2025, and Natera (NTRA), which recently grew ~36% in Q1 2025. For Precipio, a 20–35% CAGR is plausible due to its smaller size, large TAM, and value-driven product offering.

Valuation

Precipio trades at a deep discount to peers. Its current enterprise value is only ~$22M on trailing revenue of ~$18.5M, implying roughly a 1x EV/revenue multiple. By comparison, established diagnostics firms carry much higher multiples—for example, Illumina’s EV/Revenue is ~3.1× EV/Revenue, and companies like Guardant and Natera trade in the ~6–11× range. This discount is currently justified by a few factors, including the current going concern language in their audit, the microcap liquidity factor, and the negative net income. But most of those factors are about to change. As I stated above, the company will likely begin to generate free cash flow in Q3 and shortly after will begin to generate net income. Their auditors will eventually then remove their going concern language (likely early 2026), which will allow certain institutional investors to invest in the company. This will then bring in new liquidity to the stock. All these current issues will be resolved come early 2026.

Returning to the discount versus peers. This discount suggests substantial upside if Precipio executes on their next 12-month plan. Even a conservative re-rating to 2× sales (well below most peers) would drive the stock up 71% to $25/share if you assume my $27m revenue forecast is accurate. A move toward mid-range multiples (3–5× sales) implies a 2–4× upside. In other words, if Precipio achieves its growth and margin targets, a valuation re-rating is likely, providing high upside at these levels. I believe as the company executes over the next 12 months, and the Q2 results prove that their financial trajectory remains on track, that the company will trade between 2x and 3x sales, which we forecast for 2025 at a midpoint of $27m, or $25 to $38/share on a fully diluted basis, versus the current share price of $14.

info@deepsailcapital.com

This information is provided for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice or an offer or solicitation to buy or sell an interest in a private fund or any other security. An offer or solicitation of an investment in a private fund will only be made to accredited investors pursuant to a private placement memorandum and associate subscription documents. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

“Deep Sail Capital Partners” returns in this document are shown as net returns or gross returns where stated. Historical net returns assume a 1.5% and 15% management and performance fee, respectively. For Net Returns of fees and expenditures figures please reach out to the fund manager at the email info@deepsailcapital.com.

“Deep Sail Capital LLC” name was changed on April 7th 2022 from the previous name “Organon Capital LLC”.

“Deep Sail Capital Partners LP” name was changed on April 6th 2022 from the previous name “Westropp Funds LP”.

* - “Strategy Since Inception” refers to the Strategy inception date of July 2016. Deep Sail Capital Partners LP’s predecessor incubator fund, “Westropp Funds LP” pivoted from a Value Investment style to a Growth at a Reasonable Price (GARP) style fund on that date. For more details on this transition or the calculation behind the “Strategy Since Inception” returns please reach out to the fund manager at info@deepsailcapital.com.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Eli Lilly's Share Price Sheds Some Weight: This Doesn't Mean To Pile In
data.description:
August 7 was a pretty painful day for shareholders of pharmaceutical giant Eli Lilly and Company (NYSE:LLY). Shares of the company plummeted over 14% after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Interestingly, the company exceeded analysts’ estimates when it came to revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share. The firm also increased some guidance figures for the year. The drop seems to be more in relation to some late-stage trial data for its latest and upcoming oral weight loss therapy.

This goes to highlight the dangers of investing in high growth companies that trade at very high multiples. In the past, I had been very bullish on Eli Lilly, and the returns achieved during that time were strong. From the time I originally rated the company a "Buy" in June of 2023 until I subsequently downgraded it to a "Hold" in February 2024, the stock had risen by 56.2%, easily outperforming the 12.3% increase that the S&P 500 saw. But since then, things have not gone exactly according to plan. Shares are down 10.1% since downgrading it, which is significantly worse than the 26.6% increase that the market experienced. And since I reaffirmed the company as a "Hold" candidate in April of this year, shares are down 13.8% while the S&P 500 is up 16.4%.

This might seem surprising considering the business continues to grow at a rapid pace. But the problem with growth investing is that the market eventually prices in future growth prospects. Even though management increased guidance for the year, the stock is still expensive. Add on top of this the bad news that it had regarding its drug trial, and I don't think this represents a good buying opportunity for those who are focused on long-term results. At the end of the day, I think that maintaining the company as a "Hold" candidate is appropriate.

Eli Lilly's great quarter

Pretty much any way you stack it, the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a great time for shareholders of Eli Lilly. The company reported revenue for the quarter of $15.56 billion. That represents a significant increase over the $11.30 billion reported one year earlier, and it also happens to be $890 million above what analysts were hoping to see. A lot of this growth for the company continues to come from Mounjaro and Zepbound. In case you're not familiar with this, they're the same drug. The only difference is that one of them is branded for those with diabetes, while the other is branded specifically for those looking for weight loss.

During the quarter, the company generated $5.20 billion in revenue from Mounjaro. That represents an increase of 68.2% compared to the $3.09 billion the company reported one year earlier. Meanwhile, Zepbound reported a rise in sales of 172% from $1.24 billion to $3.38 billion. These continue to be great drugs that create significant shareholder value. They're also helping out a lot of people. I'm one of them. I began my own weight loss journey in the middle of 2023. In that timeframe, I have lost 101.6 pounds. Most of that has come about since I began taking Zepbound in late September of last year. From that time through today, I have lost 73.9 pounds and I continue to lose between 1 and 2 pounds each week.

There have been other growth areas for the company during this time. For instance, the company has seen immunology revenue rise by 16% from $1.08 billion to $1.26 billion during this period. And oncology revenue has grown by 11.8% from $2.16 billion to $2.41 billion. This doesn't mean that everything has been going great. As Zepbound and Mounjaro continue to dominate the weight loss industry, it's creating some cannibalization for Eli Lilly’s Trulicity. Year over year, its revenue has contracted by 12.3% from $1.25 billion to $1.09 billion. The company also saw declines when it came to Jardiance.

With the rise in revenue has come an increase in profitability. Year over year, earnings per share jumped from $3.28 to $6.29. Earnings ended up being $0.79 greater than what analysts were hoping to see. This translated to a rise in net profits from $2.97 billion to $5.66 billion. On an adjusted basis, the company saw an increase in earnings per share from $3.92 to $6.31. This happened to be $0.72 above what analysts were hoping to see. And it also translated to a rise in adjusted net income from $3.54 billion to $5.68 billion. Operating cash flow jumped from $1.47 billion to $3.09 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $2.87 billion to $5.35 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $4.72 billion to $7.50 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all growing materially. In fact, the picture is so robust that management has decided to increase guidance for revenue for the year. They now expect sales of between $60 billion and $62 billion. This compares favorably to the previous expected range of between $58 billion and $61 billion.

If everything goes according to plan, earnings per share will be between $20.85 and $22.10. This would mean about $19.32 billion in net profits at the midpoint compared to the $10.59 billion reported for 2024. Adjusted earnings per share should be between $21.75 and $22.10. The midpoint there would imply adjusted net profits of around $20.13 billion. That's up meaningfully from the $11.75 billion reported for last year. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at a similar rate, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of $25.49 billion and EBITDA of $33.03 billion.

With these estimates, you can see in the chart above how the company is valued compared to how it's valued using results from last year. Even though the trading multiple for the firm has come down considerably when we look at the picture through the lens of forward estimates, the stock is still quite pricey. This is especially true compared to other similar enterprises. In the table below, I compared Eli Lilly to five other similar firms. And what I found was that, across the board, it ended up being more expensive than any of the prospects.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Eli Lilly and Company 30.1 23.8 19.6
Novo Nordisk (NVO) 13.4 12.0 9.1
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 18.3 18.0 12.4
Merck & Co. (MRK) 12.3 10.9 9.0
AstraZeneca (AZN) 28.0 17.3 14.9
Pfizer (PFE) 12.9 9.1 9.1

Of course, as the industry leader, Eli Lilly deserves some premium to its competitors. This is especially true considering the size of the industry and the growth that it should see in the coming years. According to one source, by the year 2031, the global market for GLP-1 receptor agonists is expected to grow to around $120 billion. That represents an 11.1% annualized increase compared to what we saw in 2024. However, there are risks from other players in the space. And this strikes at the heart of why shares of Eli Lilly have fallen.

The stock fell after management disclosed data regarding a late-stage trial for its oral GLP-1 receptor agonist known as orforglipron that showed that, over a 72-week window of time, its Phase 3 ATTAIN-1 trial that consisted over 3,000 obese and overweight adults saw average weight loss of just 12.4%. That compared to the 0.9% weight loss experienced by those taking the placebo. Although this is not bad, it's important to keep in mind that, in September 2024, Novo Nordisk reported up to 13% weight loss in just 12 weeks for its oral weight loss candidate known as amycretin, which was only tested at that time in a Phase 1 trial. This means that, compared to what Novo Nordisk has to offer, orforglipron has been disappointing.

This is certainly something for investors to take into consideration. But I don't think this is a reason to panic. The fact of the matter is that the oral versions of these drugs is designed to open the door to a larger market opportunity than what the more expensive injectable drugs currently do. This is because of increased accessibility and decreased cost. However, it has long been understood that these drugs would never be as effective as those that can be injected such as Mounjaro/Zepbound, Wegovy, and Ozempic. Furthermore, Eli Lilly also has other drugs in its pipeline.

In the image above, you can see what some of these are. This includes new indications for existing drugs, as well as completely new drugs for things ranging from weight loss and diabetes to sleep apnea and breast cancer. The one drug that I think is most interesting is retatrutide. At this time, it is in Phase 3 trials for diabetes, obesity, obstructive sleep apnea, and more. That drug is currently expected to see the completion of its trial in April of next year. And according to the data provided, it is likely to be even better than Zepbound is, with up to 24.2% weight loss over 48 weeks according to Phase 2 results. Average weight loss was 17.5% at 24 weeks, which was also higher than what Zepbound has experienced.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I do believe that the future is bright for Eli Lilly. The company is growing rapidly and will continue to create value for shareholders. The market’s reaction was driven entirely by worries over competitive pressures. That could certainly cut into the company's growth as time goes on. Having said that, at the moment, the business continues to be the undeniable leader in the injectable space. Although it is entirely possible that shares could rebound following this decline, especially in light of the strong showing for the second quarter of the year, I believe that the stock is pricey as it stands. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘Hold’ candidate is the best choice.

data.title: Transocean Reports Strong Quarterly Earnings And Reduces Its Debt
data.description:
Transocean Ltd. (NYSE:RIG) has just reported sound quarterly earnings results. Also, the company’s management held a press conference where the sales guidance was raised. At the same time, the most interesting part of the conference call, in my view, was an even more radical debt reduction planned for this year and also next year. But let me go into further details about the key points of the earnings call.

Recap of my previous article

In my previous article, I wrote that Transocean’s stock was trading near 52-week lows. Its financials, however, have improved, while its backlog has remained stable. In fact, its stock was undervalued in comparison to its peers.

In my view, the stock's weakness was due to low oil prices, slow backlog growth, and a lack of market enthusiasm. At the same time, I also wrote that offshore rig supply remained tight. The day rates were also rising.

Among the upside factors for the stock, I noted the oil price recovery and the Fed’s easing. This, I argued, could make Transocean's utilization and profit margins rise, thus driving the stock price to the $10–$25 range.

The downside risks included macroeconomic uncertainty and high indebtedness. At the same time, RIG stock's low valuations and volatility could also be regarded as an upside factor in case the contract activity levels improve.

Transocean’s sales and earnings

For the past quarter, Transocean reported sales revenues of $988 million and also a net loss of $938 million, or $1.06 per diluted share.

Even though on the surface it looks like bad news, the net loss was due to net unfavorable items, including the $1.128 billion net of tax loss on impairment of assets and also the $24 million expense on debt-to-equity conversion.

So, if we consider these net unfavorable items, the Q2 2025 adjusted net income totaled $19 million.

After all, the asset impairment neither affects the company’s liquidity nor its cash flows.

That is why I prepared RIG’s quarterly sales and EBITDA graph below.

RIG’s sales and earnings

(The data are presented in millions of USDs.)

Both the company’s sales and its EBITDA seem to be in an upside trend since the end of 2022. But let me also view these profits in the context of the company’s and its competitors’ profit margins.

According to Seeking Alpha, the company’s competitors are Patterson-UTI Energy, Inc. (PTEN), Valaris (VAL), Seadrill (SDRL), Helmerich & Payne (HP), and Noble Corporation (NE). However, its closest peers are mostly considered to be Valaris, Seadrill, and Noble.

If we see the table above, we will see that RIG’s profit margins, including the gross profit and the EBITDA margins, are no worse and even better than those of its close competitors. The only exception is the net profit margin. The net profit margin, however, is worse than its peers’ due to Transocean's asset impairments.

Key points of the earnings call and my take on them

Transocean’s CEO, Keelan Adamson, said the management would cut the company’s cash costs by almost $100 million in both 2025 and 2026 thanks to lower fleet operating and maintenance expenses. Moreover, there would be additional expense reductions of about $50 million from 2026, involving the shore-based organization. In addition to the cost savings, according to Adamson, Transocean is “on track to reduce debt by more than $700 million this year".

In my view, deleveraging is a positive trend for Transocean that many analysts and investors tend to ignore. The diagram below, borrowed from Trading Economics, shows that Transocean’s debt reached its peak in 2019, nearing almost $10 billion. However, right now it is below $7 billion and is on track to decrease even further.

Transocean’s management regularly takes deleveraging measures. For example, quite recently, the company has agreed to exchange.

“$157 million in principal amount of 4% senior exchangeable bonds, issuing 59.4 million shares, with approximately $77 million in bonds outstanding.”

Deleveraging is very important for Transocean because the net cash interest expense for the third quarter alone is expected to be about $136 million. So, if the debt decreases further, it would be a great achievement for the company’s profitability, its cash flows, and its liquidity position.

Transocean’s liquidity at the end of the quarter was approximately $1.3 billion, including:

“unrestricted cash and cash equivalents of $377 million, $395 million of restricted cash, the majority of which is reserved for debt service, and $510 million of liquidity from” the company’s “undrawn credit facility.”

Transocean’s management predicts that the company’s cash at the end of the year will be between $1.45 billion and $1.55 billion, which is above the $1.3 billion reported at the end of the last quarter. This includes the effect of Transocean’s cost savings initiative. So, we can clearly see that Transocean’s balance sheet is improving.

Also, Keelan Adamson remains firm in his expectations for Transocean to achieve the 3.5x net debt to EBITDA indicator, which would allow the company to even consider “distributions to shareholders.” However, Adamson expects the company to achieve this in “late 2026.”

Also, the company’s operating performance is expected to improve both in the next quarter and in the full year of 2025. For the Q3, the sales would be between $1 billion and $1.02 billion, assuming revenue efficiency of 96.5%. The sales for the full 2025 are forecasted to be between $3.9 billion and $3.95 billion, substantially above the $3.52 billion for the full 2024.

Roderick J. Mackenzie, the company’s Executive Vice President and Chief Commercial Officer, said that Transocean’s fleet utilization would reach its bottom in the mid-80% range. In the last couple of quarters, there has been a 10%-15% decrease in day rates. As it was mentioned during the question and answer session, the offshore industry has been in the mid-to-high 400s for some time now but has recently seen a drop to the low 400s level. But it would last only for quite a short time. The market, however, would tighten by late 2026–early 2027. So, the active ultra-deepwater fleet’s utilization would be above 90%, thus pushing the day rates upwards. But for now, the management does not aim to sign many long-term contracts right now but is “much more focused on gap fillers at the moment.”

According to the management, the major demand growth drivers would be Africa, the Mediterranean Sea, and Asia. Wood Mackenzie also predicts that ultra-deepwater development capital expenditures will increase from $64 billion in 2025 to $79 billion in 2027.

Risks

It has been generally accepted here by Seeking Alpha’s analyst community that one of the main risks Transocean faces is its heavy debt. I have just mentioned above how Transocean’s deleveraging is going, which suggests that one of the main risks is disappearing.

Below, I have put an excerpt from Seeking Alpha’s website with the main data from the company’s balance sheet. The key data are also compared to Transocean’s close peers.

Unfortunately, the table above does not consider Transocean’s alternative forms of cash. At the end of the recently reported quarter, Transocean’s total liquidity was about $1.3 billion. Therefore, the net debt seems to be too high. At the same time, even if we have a quick look at the company’s competitors, we will see that their balance sheets are not impeccable either. This is especially true of Valaris with its high debt/free cash flow and long-term debt/total capital ratios.

Also, Transocean’s close peers’ total debt-to-equity ratios are quite high. True, Transocean’s debt/equity ratio is higher. But still, the offshore industry’s players’ balance sheets are not as clean as they used to be immediately after their post-pandemic re-emergence.

Another two major risks Transocean is facing are that of a recession, which can easily make oil prices crash the way they did during Covid-19, and a more general offshore industry slowdown. The latter can happen for many different reasons, including the Fed’s tighter monetary policies and the “green” revolution. However, provided the economy does not slow down and the rig market tightens like Transocean’s management has forecasted, the future for RIG seems to be bright.

Valuations

Before I dig into valuation ratios, let me have a look at Transocean’s and its close rivals’ stock price histories in the past year.

If we have a look at the graph above, we will see that Transocean’s stock price performed worse than its peers’, even though Transocean’s financial indicators are not substantially worse than those of its competitors. In fact, Transocean still remains the industry’s leader thanks to its backlog and its fleet.

Moreover, we cannot say that all of Transocean’s valuation ratios are higher than its peers. Only EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA ratios are generally higher. But the P/S, P/B, and price/cash flow ratios are lower than those of many of its competitors. So, I disagree with many analysts’ argument that RIG stock still remains high.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I would say that Transocean reported a solid set of results. Meanwhile, its financial position, including its cash reserves and debt, is improving. The management remains highly optimistic, whereas the stock is not expensive. On the flip side, the day rates are somewhat lower compared to the middle of 2024, while a few long-term contracts are announced. However, I believe this is temporary, provided oil prices increase and the global economy does not enter a prolonged recession. So, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Flagship Portfolio Performance: +26% (YTD 7/31/25)
data.description:
If you're reading this article and wondering where I have been, as this is only my fourth free site piece published so far in 2025, it has been a very busy stretch. Between managing about $1.5 million in two family accounts (so equity capital at risk as I'm excluding mandated cash) and an additional $15 million of equity capital in two family offices in a small cap value strategy (think small cap value sleeves within a larger Family Offices), as well as actively leading my Investing Group, Second Wind Capital, here on Seeking Alpha, let's just say I haven't had much spare bandwidth. In addition, my wife and I have three children (ages 7, 9, and 12) and my two boys both play summer baseball (9U and 12U), which means and has meant (as there are a few summer ball games left in the season) we've attended various tournaments (including a tournament in Waterville, ME) and dozens of regular season games.

Candidly, the second half of February 2025 - first half of April 2025, were extremely difficult periods of time when you're running money. As you might imagine, it is arguably much harder to actually manage money as opposed to run a PA (personal account). As I am not a macro analyst nor am I a market timer, I had to take the February - mid April 2025 drawdowns in stride.

Unfortunately, if people panicked in March 2025 or in early April 2025, they might have really dinged their 2025 results. It is my understanding that we experienced the fourth fastest bear market in history. That said, as everyone should be well aware, there has been a big snapback rally, especially for the Mag 7, whiz bang technology stocks, and almost anything AI related or AI adjacent. The broader market captured by the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq (QQQ) have raced to new highs. The small cap market, captured by the Russell 2000 (IWM), though, has consistently and persistently lagged its large cap and mid cap peers.

In today's piece, despite only being a small cap value and special situation investor and tactical trader, the absolute lowest returning market segment, once again, I've found a way to generate pretty good results. To that end, I'm writing to share my YTD July 31, 2025 Flagship portfolio performance. My goal is to focus more on my process and share my go-to market strategy as opposed to spending too much time getting bogged down writing a few sentences on dozens of tickers and risk having people get lost in the weeds.

Incidentally, fellow SA contributor, Erik Conley, wrote a timely piece, on August 2, 2025. Erik's article contains a number of excellent charts and macro observations. As his timely chart, see below, aptly captures, Small Cap Value has been the absolute worst place to make money on a YTD basis as well as since the October 2022 market low.

Despite playing in one of the absolute worst market segments and holding 40% to 55% of the overall portfolio value in cash, throughout the year (per my mandate), my year-to-date 2025 (through July 31, 2025) Flagship portfolio performance was +25.95%. Please note, throughout 2025, I've been mandated to maintain at least $800k in cash. And the flagship portfolio cash balance has fluctuated between $800K and $1.2 million the entire year. However, to be crystal clear, despite holding an excessive amount of cash (40% to 55% of the total portfolio held in cash), which only currently earns about 4% annualized, my performance numbers are inclusive of the cash. In other words, my YTD July 2025 total return is divided by the full denominator (including mandated cash) to calculate the results ($467,043 in total return / $1,788,578).

Enclosed below is the Fidelity portfolio performance tracker, which includes monthly cadence.

Why Small Caps Are So Tricky

As I briefly covered above, albeit in broad brush strokes, I am a small cap value and special situation investor and tactical trader. Unlike in the technology sector, which has benefited from the AI Capex spending gold rush, currently estimates to be roughly $100 billion per quarter, as there is currently an arms race between Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), the small cap segment has had very limited tailwinds. Let's face it, if you are an AI company, utility, data center company, infrastructure company tied to adding new grid capacity, or almost any company downstream of this massive and prolonged Capex super cycle, it has been an amazing period to grow your revenue, increase your margins and post bumper profits. In addition, given all of the excitement associated with the sector, investors have gotten the added benefit of increasing inflows and multiple expansion. Essentially, these companies are like Jack in the Beanstalk, hiding under the table, as they get to eat the giant crumbs that fall from the Four Giants' weekly banquet table.

Moreover, the market is very factor/ momentum oriented and perhaps because of higher interest rates, fickle consumer sentiment, and very weak inflows, the valuation gap between the Russell 1000 and Russell 2000 is at or nearing a historic peak.

As we've established above, small cap value is and has been deeply out of favor, in flows have been very weak (notably compared to large caps), interest rates are high, and sentiment has been very poor. In addition, in the sub $500 million market capitalization space, often but certainly not always, there is limited buy side sponsorship, limited, if any, sell side coverage, and most tickers usually have low average daily volume measured in shares and absolute dollars traded. These combination of factors mean that large pools of capital aren't able to invest in these companies, even if they like the valuation and a particular company's prospects. Lastly, due to the high retail ownership and very high shareholder base churn rates, as a lot of people see small caps mostly as trading vehicles, the list of reasons to avoid small caps seems to be bursting at the seams. Despite all of this, and don't get me wrong, focusing on this sector can be both exhausting and exasperating, certainly at times, I focus on the small cap value space because this is where the market is the most inefficient. If you are questing for alpha, I would agree that you want to fish in waters that are the most inefficient, as there is the greatest chance of alpha generation.

My Process

My go-to market strategy is two pronged. I have a Focus list of investments names that are usually between four to seven names. These are higher conviction investments ideas where I have what I consider to be a well thought out thesis and an attractive valuation profile. Excluding very high amounts of mandated cash, the Focus List sizing, as percentage of the overall portfolio tends to range from 50% to 65% of the capital at risk within the portfolio. The rest of the portfolio is allocated to tactical ideas. There are no exact rules and sizing ranges, within the two buckets of capital and strategies, but in order to try and generate the best returns, the Focus list names need to be concentrated and bet with conviction in order to attempt to generate strong returns.

It also goes without saying that I need to hit for a high slugging percentage and get at least one or two of the Focus list names really right. This can include aggressively adding names on irrational sell-offs, as you can have an edge because you know the companies well and have a good sense of how to value them. The way I think about the tactical portfolio is that I need to compound the other pool capital and generate incremental returns (which often include holding a core position and then having some additional trading stock) such that I can be patient, and stay invested in the Focus List ideas, and let a few good theses play out. When you're playing in value, valuation alone isn't enough and companies can remain undervalued and overlooked for long periods of time, even if the underlying businesses are executing well. This is why so many investors are focused on momentum strategies as they are unwilling to actually bet on a thesis and risk being early.

Given how difficult the first four months of 2025 were, I had to double my efforts and be very active on tactical front. And when I say tactical ideas, often I'm referring to trading stocks during earnings season or when there is major company specific piece news. As I follow upwards of 300 to 400 companies, at least from a far, and I've been doing this for many years now, my job is to synthesize a number of earnings reports and scan the news (between 6am and 9am) and from (4pm and 4:30pm) on a daily basis. My edge is my ability to quickly synthesize and can benefit from follow so many companies, again mostly companies with market capitalization sub $1 billion, that aren't widely covered and often have limited institutional (buy side sponsorship).

As you can see below, I invested the two hours to pull the Fidelity data, reformat it, and then group it to capture the YTD July 31st 2025 net realized gains. Enclosed below is a chart containing a list of every realized transaction, from January 1, 2025 - July 31, 2025.

As I said, I don't want to get side tracked or bogged down writing on dozens of tickers. Therefore, instead I will simply list the top five largest realized winners and losers.

Top Five Largest Realized Winners

  • Cineverse Corp. (CNVS): $90,240 (I added incremental trading shares in the upper $3s and at $4.50 and sold around $6.50, while maintaining a large core position).
  • Green Plains Inc. (GPRE): $42,078 (I still own a core position)
  • Cyngn Inc. (CYN): $30,937
  • The Arena Group Holdings, Inc. (AREN): $26,081
  • Draganfly Inc. (DPRO): $22,676

Top Five Largest Realized Losers

  • The Trade Desk Inc. (TTD): -$35,311
  • EVgo, Inc. (EVGO): -$24,384
  • Signing Day Sports, Inc. (SGN): -$20,158
  • ECD Automotive Design, Inc. (ECDA): -$16,315
  • Stitch Fix, Inc. (SFIX): -$15,186

As you can see in the chart above, there are many ways to play this game, and no one bats a thousand. I was wrong about 35% of the time. The idea is to be right more than you're wrong and to bat for a high slugging percentage. Also, it is a bit of an art to 'know when to hold 'em and when to fold 'em' as the late and great Kenny Rogers once sung.

As I said, today's piece is really designed just to report my results with transparency.

On the Focus List side, I don't want to share the current names per se, at least not at this time. However, I will say that far and away Cineverse Corp. has been the largest single contributor to overall YTD (July 31, 2025) results.

Through July 31, 2025, the stock has pulled back over $2 points from its 52 week high, of $7.36, struck on July 8th.

However, to be clear, I've maintain a core position, see below, and believe I have a very strong fundamental thesis here. If you tally the unrealized and realized gains from CNVS, they represent nearly 41% of the portfolio's return. This is what I mean when I say investing with high conviction and trying to hit for slugging percentage. A high slugging percentage helps offset some of the strike-out and misses.

As a deep fundamental dive into Cineverse Corp. and why I'm so bullish is beyond the scope of today's article, I reserve the right to share an update on the company, in a future SA free site piece.

Putting It All Together

Despite holding a mandated 40% to 55% of total portfolio level cash, and weathering the fourth largest bear market in history, I'm happy to share that my Flagship portfolio has returned 26% YTD through July 31, 2025. The goal of today's piece was to share some commentary on my go to market strategy. Moreover, it is my view that readers benefit when authors actually publish detailed view of their overall portfolio returns (I'm not talking about an excel spreadsheet). I would argue high level portfolio total return sharing, with monthly cadence, offers readers enough transparency to help them evaluate the ability of author to create alpha.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oddity Tech: Earnings Reaction Makes No Sense - Time To Back Up The Truck
data.description:
Thesis Review:

Oddity Tech (NASDAQ:ODD) is a purely DTC beauty company with two current brands: IL MAKIAGE and Spoiled Child. I’ve covered Oddity in the past, and it remains my highest-conviction idea—especially following its recent selloff. In short, I believe Oddity is a tremendously high-quality business operating in a great industry. Its 100% DTC distribution model gives it significant advantages over legacy competitors, particularly through its ability to easily collect customer data and maintain full control over pricing and brand image.

Additionally, Oddity boasts phenomenal financials, consistently achieving gross margins above 70%, EBITDA margins over 20%, and sustaining a long runway for 20%+ top-line growth—all while requiring minimal capital investment. These factors have enabled the company to generate exceptionally high returns on capital, a key trait I look for in the businesses I invest in.

Evidently, a business of this nature will likely trade at a premium. Yet, Oddity currently trades at a very reasonable multiple. Following its recent selloff, the stock is priced at just 13.8x NTM EV/EBITDA and 15x NTM EV/EBIT. I believe these multiples are absurdly low given the quality and growth prospects of the business—points I will elaborate on later and have addressed in previous articles. Overall, I view the recent post-earnings selloff as completely unjustified, especially considering the company’s improving fundamentals and increasingly compelling growth prospects with the launch of new brands.

Q2 Review:

Believe it or not, Oddity reported a strong second quarter—despite what a 20%+ selloff might suggest. For the quarter, Oddity delivered $241 million in revenue, exceeding the high end of their guidance at $239 million. They reported a gross margin of 72.3% versus guidance of 70.5%, and $70 million in adjusted EBITDA, above the high-end estimate of $68 million.

For the full year, Oddity raised its revenue outlook to $799–$804 million, compared to prior guidance of $790–$798 million. They also maintained their gross margin guidance and raised their adjusted EBITDA guidance to $160–$162 million from $157–$161 million.

The only slight negative was their Q3 gross margin guidance of 68%, which came in just below consensus. However, I find this largely irrelevant, as they maintained full-year gross margin guidance—and Oddity has a consistent track record of sandbagging guidance to easily beat estimates. This is evidenced by their nine straight quarters of exceeding expectations and raising guidance.

Earnings Call:

Given the strong beat on their reported numbers, one might assume that the earnings call must have gone poorly for the stock to sell off. However, while the interpretation of an earnings call is inherently subjective, I found it to be nothing but positive—with several extremely encouraging takeaways.

First, in addressing the slightly below-consensus gross margin guidance for Q3, Oddity’s CFO explained the reasoning:

"So on gross margin, as you know, this is not a metric that our teams manage to. They manage to DC margin, which is contribution margin, it's gross margin after media spend" (Q2 earnings call).

"We've also, as you know, since you've been covering us since our IPO, we've overdelivered on that metric consistently every quarter as a result of wanting to embed enough conservatism to give the teams flexibility and not be in a position to disappoint the Street" (Q2 earnings call).

Evidently, Oddity’s bottom-line margin remains well on track to meet its long-term objective of 20% EBITDA margins, and the “below-consensus” gross margin guidance clearly reflects a conservative approach.

Next, in terms of continued growth prospects, Oddity emphasized the strength of its international expansion strategy:

"Very happy with our first half performance. This is a business that could easily be as large as our U.S. business. As you know, for our competitors, it's something like 70% of their business comes from international markets. And everything that we see is that the markets behave very similarly outside the U.S. to what we have already accomplished in the U.S."

Just to give you -- put a little bit of numbers around it, in the first half, sales outside the U.S. grew over 40%, that's around $85 million. Of that $85 million, $75 million were markets that were already established in. So for example, U.K., Australia. But we have around $10 million from these new kind of testing markets where we see a lot of potential" (Q2 earnings call).

Evidently, Oddity has an extremely powerful lever for continued growth through international expansion. Considering their ambition to sustain 20%+ revenue growth and strong EBITDA margins well into the future, expanding internationally is a key avenue to achieving that goal.

Furthermore, another core pillar of the Oddity investment thesis is their exceptionally strong repeat rates, driven by their robust data advantage—something management touched on again:

"Repeat continues to increase as a percentage of the business year-over-year. And as we look at our 12-month repeat cohorts, those remain very strong, over 100% and performing well for both brands" (Q2 earnings call).

Repeat rates exceeding 100% are remarkable, giving Oddity deep insight into product demand while also demonstrating their ability to deliver exceptional products that keep customers coming back.

Lastly, a key catalyst and growth opportunity for the business is Brand 3—a medical-grade telehealth platform. Management discussed their strong conviction in the brand’s value proposition and, notably, shared that current guidance does not factor in any revenue contribution from Brand 3. This creates significant upside potential for Oddity to exceed estimates.

"One thing that I would say about Brand 3 and our distinctive approach there mostly is around personalization. Our team spent almost 2 years developing the critical personalized treatments and developed almost 25 customer cohorts with unique treatment combination based on our testing. It shows material, material improvement in satisfaction. Some numbers are above 50% comparing to what exists in the market" (Q2 earnings call).

Pound the Table Opportunity

In essence, I’m “pounding the table” and exclaiming: buy Oddity. To review, there are a few key attributes that make this a truly exceptional investment opportunity—traits rarely found in other businesses:

1. 100% DTC distribution: Oddity’s fully direct-to-consumer model, bypassing traditional retail channels like Ulta and Sephora, gives it a distinct advantage. This model allows Oddity to collect detailed data on its consumers and maintain complete control over its brand image. Traditional beauty companies sell the vast majority of their products through retail—either in their own stores or through third-party partners—which severely limits the data they can gather. In contrast, Oddity’s online-only distribution enables deep data collection that it can leverage across various aspects of the business. Additionally, by avoiding third-party retailers, Oddity retains full pricing power and further enhances its visibility into consumer behavior.

2. “Tech-first” approach: Oddity’s data advantage is amplified through its integration of AI technologies, such as machine learning models. These tools allow the company to meaningfully improve the customer experience, drive high repeat and retention rates, and identify unmet consumer needs—ultimately enabling the launch of entirely new brands that address major customer pain points.

3. Moat: I believe it is extremely difficult for legacy beauty businesses to replicate what Oddity does. Because traditional companies rely heavily on retail channels, they inherently lack the data visibility that Oddity enjoys. As a result, they face greater challenges in retaining customers, creating seamless online shopping experiences, and launching new products—all of which carry significantly more risk.

4. Great industry with secular tailwinds: The beauty industry, in my view, is inherently attractive. Beauty products have high margins due to low formulation costs, consumers exhibit strong willingness to pay given the perceived benefits (looking better), and demand is largely non-discretionary. Moreover, with only 25% of beauty sales in the U.S. currently taking place online, the ongoing digital shift creates a major tailwind for Oddity as a natural beneficiary.

Valuation

Evidently, the story behind Oddity appears highly attractive. However, it's crucial that the narrative is supported by the numbers in a financial model. To that end, I modeled Oddity extensively to arrive at a final valuation—starting first with a detailed revenue build.

Given management’s guidance that Spoiled Child would generate approximately $200 million in revenue, I used this figure to back out the implied revenue for IL MAKIAGE. For future projections, considering the company’s commitment to a 20%+ growth algorithm and the strong outlook for its core brands, I modeled revenue deceleration for both Spoiled Child and IL MAKIAGE at a very gradual pace.

For Brand 3, I estimated $7 million in revenue for 2025, based on its expected Q4 launch and early-stage potential. I applied a growth trajectory similar to Spoiled Child’s, though more conservative. I followed a similar approach for Brand 4, assuming its launch in 2026.

For the cost build, I made straightforward assumptions without introducing any significant changes. I assumed a modest improvement in gross margin over time as Oddity continues to optimize its supply chain and benefit from pricing power. Additionally, I kept stock-based compensation flat as a percentage of revenue. For SG&A, I modeled a 1% improvement each year as a percentage of revenue—an assumption I consider extremely conservative given the high operating leverage inherent in the beauty industry, particularly when marketing is executed effectively.

I calculated Oddity’s WACC to use as the discount rate. Since the company’s issued debt is in the form of a convertible note with a 0% coupon, I treated it as equity and excluded debt from the calculation. I applied a standard market risk premium of 7% and assumed an equity beta of 1.2, which is the average across their comp set. This results in a cost of equity of 12.7%.

For the valuation I did a standard build starting at revenue and getting to free cash flow.

For the perpetuity growth method, I assumed a 5% growth rate which is high but necessary given the tremendous growth potential of the business.

For the exit multiple method, I assumed an 18x exit multiple given the tremendously high quality nature of this business, sustainability of their revenues, and continuous growth prospects after the 5 year window I modeled for the business. Lastly, due to the recent convertible note that Oddity issued, calculating their net debt is difficult, however, the company now maintains a cash & cash equivalents position of $678.7 million while the value of their debt (primarily their 0% coupon convertible note) is $582.626 million. Therefore, the business's net debt value is -$96 million.

When putting it all together and averaging the outputs from both methods, the implied equity value is approximately $81 per share—representing nearly a 40% premium to where Oddity trades today. Moreover, I believe many of the assumptions used in the model were overly conservative, and the perpetuity growth method tends to undervalue “growth” stocks, further weighing down the valuation. Overall, I believe this reinforces the view that Oddity is an extremely compelling investment opportunity.

Risk Factors + Final Thoughts:

Although Oddity is an enticing opportunity in my opinion, I believe there are a few key risk factors. First, the business would suffer if the launch of their new brands is not successful or requires more capital than intended. I find this unlikely given the data they have to launch the new brands, the time spend developing them, and their history launching new brands. Furthermore, if competition emerges from other businesses starting purely online beauty businesses it may present a challenge to Oddity's moat. Overall, I believe the risks to Oddity are minimal, and it is the epitome of a wonderful business at a fair price.
